lessons.title: What Is a Remote? Understanding Named Repository URLs
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain what a remote is and understand that remotes are named aliases for repository URLs.
lessons.notes: Example: `origin` usually points to GitHub repo. Multiple remotes possible (e.g., `upstream` for canonical repository). Explain naming convention.

lessons.title: Configuring Remotes with `git remote`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to view, add, and remove remote configurations using `git remote` commands.
lessons.notes: Show: `git remote` (list), `git remote -v` (show URLs), `git remote add upstream <url>`, `git remote remove upstream`.

lessons.title: Cloning a Repository
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to clone a repository from GitHub and understand what gets copied.
lessons.notes: When cloning: entire object database copied, `origin` remote created automatically, default branch checked out. Much faster than downloading ZIP.

lessons.title: Pushing Commits to a Remote with `git push`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to push local commits to a remote repository and set up tracking branches.
lessons.notes: Show: `git push origin main` (push main to origin). Then `git push -u origin feature-auth` (push and set tracking). Explain `-u` flag.

lessons.title: Understanding Remote-Tracking Branches
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain remote-tracking branches and understand their relationship to local branches.
lessons.notes: Remote-tracking branches like `origin/main` are read-only pointers to the remote's state as of last fetch. They're not your working branches.

lessons.title: Fetching Remote Changes without Merging
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to fetch remote changes and inspect them before merging into local branches.
lessons.notes: Fetch updates remote-tracking branches but doesn't change your local branches. Safe way to see what's new before integrating.

lessons.title: Pulling Remote Changes with `git pull`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to pull remote changes (fetch + merge) and understand the implications for local history.
lessons.notes: Explain: `git pull` = `git fetch` + `git merge`. If remote has new commits and you do too, you get merge or conflict.

lessons.title: The GitHub Workflow: Clone, Branch, Push, Pull Request
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to apply the complete GitHub workflow from cloning a repository through opening a pull request.
lessons.notes: Workflow: (1) clone repo, (2) create feature branch, (3) make commits, (4) push to GitHub, (5) open PR in web UI, (6) merge after review.
